Content Signatures

It is smart to verify that content used by a title is secure and has not been tampered with, both for the sake of robustness and to prevent attacks against the security of the title and the console itself. Additionally, there are certification requirements concerning a title's prevention of unauthorized alteration of content installed to the hard drive. Because protecting data is a complex problem, requiring specialized tools and runtime validation code, the XDK provides content packaging tools and runtime functions that ease the process of securing installable content, while still allowing titles to make data access optimizations based on the titles' specific data formats.

Not all content installed on the hard disk will be installed by game titles. This content may be installed by users from a Dashboard download session, by demo titles, or by other means. Having a standard installation format facilitates the security of installed data, while making it easy for content to be securely installed by an installer that has no internal knowledge of the structure or format of the content itself.

The content signature tools and functions provide a simple, standard, reliable, and secure way of creating, storing, retrieving, and validating signatures for installed content files.

Content Signature Data

Each package of installed content has a single, signed file of metadata. This file contains information necessary to describe the content, as well as all of the signatures used to validate the integrity of the installed content.

Two formats for signature data are supported by the tools and signature functions: literal and automatic. Literal signature data is any arbitrary chunk of binary data (such as a non-keyed hash) that can be used to verify the integrity of the content. This data is computed and verified using any algorithm of the developer's choosing. The signature file serves only to store the signature data. Automatic signatures are computed by the content build tool as hashes of the data to be signed. Each automatic signature is computed over a contiguous run of binary data within a single file. Each file can have an arbitrary number of signatures, with any mixture of literal and non-literal signatures.

Signatures can also be marked as named or anonymous. Every signature is given an index number in the signature file. A named signature can be located at runtime by file name, byte offset, and run length, or by index number. An anonymous signature can only be located by index number.

Building Content Packages

The XBldSigs tool computes content signatures. XBldSigs takes a collection of files and constructs a single metadata file that describes the content and contains content signatures. This process of generating the signatures and other metadata with XBldSigs is for offline titles. Xbox Live titles should use BuildOffer.

XBldSigs scans all of the files under a directory and computes signature information for each file. Signature information will be generated for each file by calling each specified signature provider, in order, until one of them indicates that it has calculated appropriate signature data for that file. If none of the signature providers recognizes the file, that file will receive a single, non-literal signature over its entire contents.

Signature providers can be file lists or signature DLLs. File lists (which are not yet supported), direct the tool to generate non-literal, named signatures over specific byte ranges within specific files. Signature DLLs provide title developers a way to determine how to partition file data into signatures without knowing beforehand what byte ranges are to be signed. For each file, XBldSigs calls the CalculateSignatures function in each signature DLL until a DLL returns TRUE, indicating that the file was processed.

The signature DLL calls the XRegisterSignature function to indicate that a signature should be added for the specified file.

See the XBldSigs documentation for details about signature DLLs and creating content metadata.

Installation of Content

Installing packaged content offerings is typically handled by the content download installer or a DVD-based package installer. However, titles may want to perform manual or custom installation or choose to verify content using a signature scheme of their own.

A title installing content by copying individual files may call XInstallContentSignatures to install and resign the content metadata file after copying all content files to the installation directory. XInstallContentSignatures copies the metadata, recalculates any automatic (non-literal) content signatures, resigns the metadata file, then writes the resulting metadata file to the content installation directory. A content download installer itself would likely call XInstallContentSignatures as the final step in installing a content package. The content signatures are then available for access through the XLoadContentSignatures function.

If a title doesn't want to verify content using a scheme built around the content signature data functions, it can use an algorithm and scheme of its own devising. To do so, it can create a simple metadata file that contains no signatures by calling XCreateContentSimple. XCreateContentSimple creates a minimal content metadata file that contains no content signatures, and writes the file to the content installation directory. This metadata file makes the content visible to the Xbox Dashboard and the content enumeration functions. However, since the metadata file contains no signatures, the title must verify content using its own scheme when first installing content and before each subsequent use of the installed content.

Titles can use the XGetContentInstallLocation or XGetContentInstallLocationFromIDs function to determine the correct content installation directory for a given content package.

Installing Content on Debug Consoles

The XbInstCont tool will install content on debug consoles.

xbinstcont [-x xboxname] [-t titleid] contentroot

This tool installs one or more content directory trees onto the target system. Each group of content is installed for the title ID specified when the content was created, unless the -t option is specified, in which case it is installed for the specified title ID. Each content directory must contain, at its root, a file called ContentMeta.xbx, which is the signature file output from XBldSigs. If a given piece of content is already installed on the target system, XbInstCont first deletes the old content (after first confirming with the user) and then installs the new content.

Runtime Verification of Content

Runtime verification of content is accomplished by loading signature data for the content offering, then retrieving signatures from the signature data and comparing them against signatures computed over the currently installed content. If the signatures match, the content is verified as complete and unmodified. If the signatures do not match, the content is invalid and must not be used.

The XLoadContentSignatures function loads the signature data for a specified content installation and verifies the integrity of the signature data itself. XLoadContentSignatures requires as parameters the title identifier of the title that installed the content, as well as the installation directory that contains the content. The installation directory is obtained either when content is enumerated with the XFindFirstContent and XFindNextContent functions, or by specifying the content offering identifier in a call to XGetContentInstallLocationFromIDs. Calling XLoadContentSignatures opens the signature data and returns a handle that can then be used to extract specific signatures from the signature data.

Signatures in the signature data are stored by index and possibly by file name as well. To retrieve a specific signature from the signature data by index, call XLocateSignatureByIndex and specify the signature data handle and index of the specific signature desired. Signatures that were stored with a file name can be retrieved by calling XLocateSignatureByName, which allows retrieving signatures that were computed over an entire file or computed over portions of data within the file.

Once a signature for a given file or portion of file has been retrieved, that signature should be compared with a signature computed over the installed content data. The XCalculateContentSignature function calculates a signature over the specified range of data, using the same algorithm used by XBldSigs to calculate automatic signatures. If literal signatures were stored for the data range (or file), then the title must compute the signature over the installed content data itself, using whatever title-specific algorithm it used to compute the signature stored in the signature data.

After the correct, stored signature has been retrieved and the signature calculated over the currently installed content, a simple binary compare of the two signatures will indicate whether the installed content is complete and unmodified, and thus can be safely used.

When done accessing signature data, close the open signature data handle with a call to XCloseContentSignatures.

Usage Scenarios

A title developer may want to produce content that is downloadable (and thus installed by a generic installer) and that is protected, but may not want to write any complex tools to generate signature information. To accomplish this, the developer can simply run XBldSigs with no DLL or file list arguments, and produce a package with a strong set of signatures. At runtime, the developer need only call the runtime verification functions, passing the entire file contents to the signature computation function (XCalculateContentSignature), to verify the integrity of the data. This mechanism involves minimal work on the developer's part.

To make the verification process more efficient and equally strong, the title developer may elect to break down file contents into piecewise signatures. This eliminates the need to read the entire file contents and compute a hash over the entire file before using it. Additionally, the developer might use anonymous signatures if the list of files or file sections is known ahead of time. Doing so reduces the runtime overhead of signature validation because the name table need not be loaded.

Alternatively, the title developer need not use any of the content signature scheme described above, but instead provide some other method to verify the integrity of installed content. The developer copies an arbitrary set of files to the appropriate location for installed content, presumably using XCalculateSignatureBegin during the copying, and then calls XCreateContentSimple to make the content visible to the content enumeration functions and the Xbox Dashboard. The title is responsible for securely storing and verifying signatures against the installed content before each use of that content.
